A Zipper Patent Sat in a Garage for 40 Years. Now It’s Real.

Back in 1985, an electrical engineer at Polaroid named Bill Freeman had an idea for a three-sided zipper. He patented it and kept the prototype for nearly four decades.

Researchers finally took the concept seriously. The result is a three-dimensional printed fastener that can turn a flexible structure into a rigid, load-bearing beam.

The zipper joins three independent flexible strips into a triangular rod the moment it is zipped. Unzip it, and the structure becomes soft again.

Potential applications include camping equipment, medical devices, robotic limbs, and structures that need to be compact during transport.

Credit belongs to the engineer who recognized the idea decades before manufacturing tools caught up with it.
